petitioner_background: The petitioner is a business executive in the television industry, serving as the president of strategy and planning international business of a major television company. He has been employed as a senior executive at the company for more than 11 years and has led its expansion throughout Asia and into Latin America, overseeing 600 employees across the United States. He sought classification as an individual of extraordinary ability under EB-1A (INA section 203(b)(1)(A)). The Nebraska Service Center Director denied his Form I-140, finding he met only one of the ten initial evidentiary criteria.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence that his company won the gold award for marketing in 2009 in the category of 'Media/Content/Internet Provider.' He submitted a letter from an individual identifying herself as president of the awarding organization, stating she met the petitioner when he created and launched a successful network in the Middle East, and that the awards are 'the Oscars of the Marketing and Advertising world.' The letter stated the petitioner and his team were awarded in 2009 due to 'his direct marketing campaigns and success of the network.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted the letter did not appear to be on letterhead and the record did not contain corroborating evidence about the author, and that the awarding organization's website did not identify her as president. The AAO found the record did not show what involvement the petitioner had in overseeing the media, content, internet provider, or direct marketing campaign aspects of the company, or how this constitutes national or international acclaim to him personally. The AAO noted the awarding organization's website indicates there are multiple categories including 40+ national programs, and the record did not demonstrate the level of prestige and acclaim one enjoys from receiving this specific award or what role the petitioner played to receive it. Discussed in final merits only.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted publications that quote him and reference his job title, including articles about company initiatives. He submitted articles reporting on his employment change when he became president of the network, including a page published by Variety. He submitted a 2016 Wall Street Journal blog post about his work, quoting him several times regarding the company's goal of taking Indian productions to Spanish-speaking audiences. He also submitted a letter from a Business Editor stating a colleague wrote the article, that the petitioner 'is the leading authority in the industry,' and that the editor had known the petitioner for over 15 years and sought him out for advice on the film and television market in India and the Middle East.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that most publications quote the petitioner and reference his job title, but the focus is about company initiatives, not the petitioner himself. Citing Noroozi v. Napolitano, published material must be about the petitioner relating to his work in the field, not just about his employer, and it is insufficient that the material merely mentions the petitioner. The brief announcements arising from a single employment change did not rise to the level of sustained national or international acclaim. The Wall Street Journal article was about the company and related to acclaim to the company, not the petitioner. The Business Editor's letter spoke to one individual's high opinion but did not state how often he reached out to the petitioner or demonstrate how consultations resulted in publications supporting acclaim, and there was no evidence that other journalists regularly interviewed him for similar reasons.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence demonstrating he served as a judge for the 2nd and 3rd editions of a competition, specifically as a judge for the special class group TV Drama. The record contained a certificate from the organization thanking the petitioner for participating as a judge for this competition, along with a dark photograph of the petitioner and another individual holding something framed.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director held that the judging evidence could not be verified and noted the dark photograph. However, the AAO found that the certificate regarding his participation as a judge for the group TV Drama sufficiently establishes that he participated as a judge of the work of others, and therefore met the judging criterion. In the final merits determination, the AAO noted the record did not show to what extent, if any, he received acclaim for being a judge.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The record demonstrates the petitioner has been employed as a senior executive at the company for more than 11 years and has led its expansion throughout Asia and into Latin America. A letter from the Director of Human Resources at a subsidiary confirmed he was promoted to president of strategy and planning international business in 2018 and oversees 600 employees across the United States. A letter from the CEO stated the petitioner directly directs 17 stations and networks around the world. The president of the legal department stated the petitioner 'handles over $250 million dollars in the U.S. and close to $10.3 billion dollars globally' and that under his direct supervision, the company created new networks in the Middle East, UAE, India and Pakistan, Russia, Indonesia, South Africa, France, Germany, countries throughout South America, Vietnam, the Philippines, China, and the United States. Letters from the head of another company division and an executive vice president described him as 'the architect' of an entire network expansion and noted the company 'tremendously prospered under his leadership.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the record establishes that the petitioner has been employed as a senior executive for more than 11 years and has led expansion throughout Asia and into Latin America, and that the company has a distinguished reputation. The AAO concluded he meets the leading or critical role criterion. In the final merits determination, however, the AAO noted that while his leadership success was apparent, the letters referred to the company's acclaim, not his personal acclaim, and he had not shown that this work led to acclaim to him personally or that he received acclaim on account of establishing the network expansion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: On appeal, the petitioner submitted additional documentation from the U.S. Department of Labor and PayScale reflecting salaries of individuals in similar positions. The record contains an employment contract for his position confirming his base salary, a pay statement from December 2017, and documentation demonstrating the significant promotion he received.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director concluded the petitioner had not provided sufficient evidence to establish his salary was high in relation to others in the field. The AAO found that when viewed together, the evidence establishes that the petitioner's salary is high in relation to others in the field under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(ix), and therefore he met this criterion. In the final merits determination, the AAO noted the evidence does not demonstrate he has received acclaim on account of his salary.

outcome_reasoning: Although the AAO found the petitioner met three of the initial evidentiary criteria (judging, leading or critical role, and high salary), in the final merits determination the AAO concluded the record does not sufficiently demonstrate sustained national or international acclaim placing him among the small percentage at the top of his field. The evidence regarding awards did not show the petitioner's personal role or level of prestige of the award. Published material was about the company, not the petitioner personally. The judging evidence did not show acclaim received for serving as a judge. While leadership success was apparent, letters referred to the company's acclaim rather than the petitioner's personal acclaim. The high salary evidence did not demonstrate acclaim on account of salary. Therefore, the petitioner did not establish eligibility as an individual of extraordinary ability.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)

USCIS Policy Memorandum PM-602-0005.1, Evaluation of Evidence Submitted with Certain Form I-140 Petitions; Revisions to the Adjudicator's Field Manual (AFM) Chapter 22.2, AFM Update AD11-N10 (Dec. 22, 2010)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO noted that a letter from a purported president of an awarding organization did not appear to be on letterhead, the record did not contain corroborating evidence about the author, and the organization's website did not identify her as president — raising credibility concerns about this evidence. The Director had also noted that judging experience evidence could not be verified and contained a dark photograph, though the AAO ultimately found other evidence sufficient for the judging criterion.
